coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent sensor based on sulfur nanodots encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 for ultrasensitive detection of tartrazine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Haijian
Wang, Miao
Huang, Guofu
coredata.dc:description: A new composite material (SDs@ZIF-8) was synthesized by integrating sulfur nanodots (SDs) into metal-organic frameworks (ZIF-8) through a facile one-step self-assembly strategy. The obtained SDs@ZIF-8 has not only the high adsorption performance of ZIF-8 but also the superior fluorescence characteristics of SDs. The composite featured good dispersibility, stable structure as well as excellent fluorescence in water solution, and can be used as an ideal fluorescent sensor for tartrazine detection. Due to the high specific surface area and adsorption performance of ZIF-8, the prepared composite material can significantly enrich tartrazine, further enhancing the sensitivity of analysis. The fluorescence of SDs @ZIF-8 composite can be effectively quenched by tartrazine through the inner filter effect. The sensing technique exhibited exceptional sensitivity, as evidenced by its impressive detection limit of 6.5 nM across a broad linear range spanning from 0.02 to 90 μM. In addition to its high sensitivity, the technique displayed rapid response times and excellent selectivity. Moreover, the fluorescent sensing technology we developed has been employed successfully for the detection of tartrazine in real samples, which is expected to promote the development of the food safety industry.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of tartrazine in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscope pictures of pure ZIF-8(A) and SDs@ZIF-8(B).
XRD spectra of pure ZIF-8 and SDs@ZIF-8.
Infrared spectra of SDs, ZIF-8 and SDs@ZIF-8 composites.
Fluorescence emission spectra of SDs@ZIF-8, SDs and ZIF-8 under excitation at 350 nm. The illustration reveals images of SDs@ZIF-8 suspensions under natural light (a) and 365 nm UV light (b).
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of SDs@ZIF-8 suspension after adding different concentrations of tartrazine and (B) the linear relationship between F0/F and tartrazine concentration.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of SDs@ZIF-8 mixed with different substances (B)The influence of interfering substances that may coexist. From 1 to 14 in order K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, HCO- 3, Cl-, glucose, ascorbic acid, citric acid, lactose, fructose, malachite green, bright blue, amaranth. Conditions: SDs@ZIF-8, 30 mg/mL; pH = 7.0; tartrazine, 40 μM; other substances, 80 μM.
The fluorescence emission spectra of SDs@ZIF-8 overlap with the UV–vis absorption spectra of tartrazine.
The synthesis of SDs@ZIF-8 and its utilization in tartrazine assay.
Analysis of tartrazine in actual samples.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent sensor based on sulfur nanodots encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 for ultrasensitive detection of tartrazine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Environment, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Shouguang 262700, Weifang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Environment
Weifang University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Environment, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Shouguang 262700, Weifang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of SDs@ZIF-8 and its utilization in tartrazine assay.

A new composite material (SDs@ZIF-8) was synthesized by integrating sulfur nanodots (SDs) into metal-organic frameworks (ZIF-8) through a facile one-step self-assembly strategy. The obtained SDs@ZIF-8 has not only the high adsorption performance of ZIF-8 but also the superior fluorescence characteristics of SDs. The composite featured good dispersibility, stable structure as well as excellent fluorescence in water solution, and can be used as an ideal fluorescent sensor for tartrazine detection. Due to the high specific surface area and adsorption performance of ZIF-8, the prepared composite material can significantly enrich tartrazine, further enhancing the sensitivity of analysis. The fluorescence of SDs @ZIF-8 composite can be effectively quenched by tartrazine through the inner filter effect. The sensing technique exhibited exceptional sensitivity, as evidenced by its impressive detection limit of 6.5 nM across a broad linear range spanning from 0.02 to 90 μM. In addition to its high sensitivity, the technique displayed rapid response times and excellent selectivity. Moreover, the fluorescent sensing technology we developed has been employed successfully for the detection of tartrazine in real samples, which is expected to promote the development of the food safety industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tartrazine, a synthetic azo dye, is widely utilized in food production, processing, and transportation to enhance the visual appeal of food items [1]. Despite its prevalent use in the food industry, numerous studies have reported potential health risks associated with the excessive consumption of tartrazine [2]. Evidence suggests that tartrazine can induce DNA damage and disrupt DNA synthesis, thereby causing fetal malformations. Furthermore, excessive intake of tartrazine may lead to the formation of carcinogens and increase the risk of subcutaneous sarcomas, liver cancer, and colorectal cancer [3,4]. The maximum content of tartrazine in food is set at 50 mg/kg by the World Health Organization and the Food and Agriculture Organization [5]. Therefore, to safeguard human health, it is urgent to develop an effective and reliable analytical protocol to monitor the level of tartrazine in food. Presently, the typical approaches used for the detection of tartrazine include UV spectrophotometry [6,7], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], electrochemical method and capillary electrophoresis [9–11]. Nevertheless, most of these methods require tedious sample pretreatment, expensive equipment, strict operating conditions and long analysis time, which limit their further applications. Fortunately, the analysis method based on fluorescence sensor is favored by researchers because of its simplicity, rapidity and economy [12–16].

Recently, as a new alternative optical nanomaterial, sulfur nanodots have been extensively explored because of their good fluorescent performance, good biocompatibility, excellent antibacterial ability and low toxicity [17–20]. Furthermore, sulfur nanodots can be easily prepared from sublimed sulfur. These features indicate that sulfur nanodots have potential applications in the field of fluorescence analysis [21,22]. As far as we know, however, there are still relatively few studies on sulfur nanodots-based fluorescent sensor showing superior sensitivity and specificity to target molecule.

Metal-organic framework materials are a type of crystalline porous material composed of organic ligands and inorganic metal ions or clusters. These materials exhibit exceptional properties such as high porosity, large specific surface area, and unique pore size distribution, which render them promising candidates for a diverse range of applications [23–25]. As a sub-class of metal–organic framework materials, Zeolite-imidazole framework materials (ZIFs) are similar in structure to zeolite-based molecular sieves, so their characteristics are also similar to those of molecular sieves. Besides, compared with molecular sieves, ZIFs have higher thermal and chemical stability [26–28]. These excellent capabilities make ZIFs promising candidates for use in the fields of energy conversion [29,30], gas storage and separation [31–33], chemical sensing [34–36], drug delivery and catalysis [37–39]. Nowadays, in order to obtain special hybrid materials with outstanding properties, an integration technique has been designed to integrate different kinds of nanoparticles within MOFs [40–42]. For example, Xu et al. used one-pot method to synthesize carbon dots embedded in metal–organic framework material, and by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) into the framework structure of ZIF-8 zeolite, the composite retained the advantages of both CDs and ZIF-8 and was applied to the sensitive sensing of quercetin [43]. Inspired by this study, we attempted to combine SDs into ZIF-8 to fabricate fluorescent composites with remarkable optical sensing performance (SDs@ZIF-8). The hybrid composite is expected to possess both the fluorescence properties of SDs and the adsorption capabilities of ZIF-8, and can be employed to detect target substances.

In the present work, we constructed a new fluorescent sensor (SDs@ZIF-8) by embedding sulfur nanodots into the ZIF-8 via a facile one-pot approach. The obtained composites featured both the high adsorption efficiency of ZIF-8 and the exceptional fluorescence performances of sulfur nanodots. Thus, ZIF-8 was utilized as an adsorbent to seize and accumulate analytes, while SDs were used as fluorescent indicators to selectively and sensitively detect target molecules. Interestingly, the absorption band of tartrazine overlaps well with the emission band of sulfur nanodots, so tartrazine can effectively quench the fluorescence of sulfur nanodots. As a result, a novel fluorescence sensor for tartrazine was constructed using the prepared SDs@ZIF-8 composite. Here, our most important innovation is the integration of the advantages of ZIF-8 and SDs to obtain new composite materials. And the composite materials were used as sensor for the sensitive detection of tartrazine for the first time. The developed sensor exhibits a broad linear range spanning from 0.02 μM to 90 μM, with a remarkably low detection limit of 6.5 nM. In addition, the sensor was successfully utilized for the accurate detection of tartrazine in diverse food samples.

Sublimed sulfur, sodium hydroxide, polyethylene glycol (PEG-400) and hydrogen peroxide were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2-methylimidazole and zinc nitrate hexahydrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals used in the experiment were analytical grade and no additional purification was required.

The preparation method of sulfur nanodots was similar to that reported in previous literature [44]. 1.4 g of sublimation sulfur, 4.0 g of sodium hydroxide and 3 mL of PEG-400 were added to a three-necked round bottom flask containing 50 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was heated and stirred at 70 °C for 72 h to allow gradual dissolution of the sublimated sulfur powder. During this process, the color of the solution changed progressively from red-brown to yellow. Subsequently, different amounts of hydrogen peroxide (4.1 wt%) were added to the above solution under stirring until a clear colorless solution was yielded. Under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp, the solution emitted strong blue fluorescence, suggesting the formation of sulfur nanodots.

SDs@ZIF-8 composites were obtained based on the approach reported in the reference with slight alteration [43,45]. Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.2933 g) and 2-methylimidazole (0.6489 g) were put into 40 mL of ultrapure aqueous solution, then 1 mL of SDs solution was immediately added to the mixture and the reaction was stirred at ambient temperature for 1 h (ZIF-8 was prepared in the absence of SDs). After completion of the reaction, the resulting products were separated by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting solids were then washed several times with ultrapure water and subsequently dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain the desired product.

As part of the standard procedure, SDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite suspensions (30 mg/mL) and tartrazine standard solutions were prepared with PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0). Afteradding500 μLof various concentrations of tartrazine solution to 500 μL of SDs@ZIF-8suspension,themixture was shakenthoroughlyand incubated at ambienttemperaturefor 2 min.Withanexcitation wavelength of 350 nm and a slit width of 5 nm, a series of fluorescence spectra wereobtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of tartrazine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Orange juice, cola drinks and hard candy were bought from local store. These food samples were pretreated before testing. For orange juice and cola drinks, they were first heated to remove gases such as carbon dioxide. Then, the beverage was diluted 10 times with ultrapure water. Finally, the diluted solution was purified by a 0.22 μm filter for further analysis. For hard candies, they were first mashed into powder. Then, 0.1 g of candies were dissolved in 10.0 mL of ultrapure water, mixed thoroughly, and the supernatants were collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The procedure for the determination of tartrazine in the above-treated food samples was the same as that mentioned in Section 2.4.

The preparation of SDs@ZIF-8 crystal was shown in Scheme 1 .Firstly, sulfur quantum dots emitting blue fluorescence were synthesized by hydrogen peroxide-assisted top-down method.Then SDs were added to the growth environment of ZIF-8 crystals to realize its merged assembly process. To verify the successful synthesis of SDs@ZIF-8 complexes, a number of characterizations were performed. Transmissionelectron microscopy(TEM) was used to observe the dimensions and shape of thecomposites.Pure ZIF-8 (Fig. 1 A) was hexagonal and approximately 286 nm in size. Interestingly, the shape and dimension of the SDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 1B) composites were essentially the same as those of pure ZIF-8. XRD was used to characterize their crystal structures. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the spectra of pure ZIF-8 and SDs@ZIF-8 were almost coincident. These results indicated that the encapsulation of SDs did not affect the skeleton structure of ZIF-8. To provide further evidence of the successful formation of SDs@ZIF-8, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that the SDs@ZIF-8 complex has numerous characteristic absorption peaks related to SDs and pure ZIF-8. Furthermore, the prepared SDs@ZIF-8 were dispersed in aqueous solution and appeared opalescent under natural light (Fig. 4 a), while emitting strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 4b). As exhibited in Fig. 4, under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, SDs@ZIF-8 displayed bright fluorescence emission at 426 nm, which is almost identical to the emission spectrum of SDs. However, pure ZIF-8 exhibits no fluorescence emission peak, indicating that the blue fluorescence of the complex materials is caused more by SDs than by ZIF-8, and SDs encapsulated in ZIF-8 still have excellent fluorescence performance.

To assess the possibility of SDs@ZIF-8 as a fluorescent sensor, we studied the effect of various conditions, including time and salt concentration, on the fluorescence stability of the material. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of SDs@ZIF-8 remained stable under continuous exposure for 120 min (20 min interval) and in a wide NaCl concentration range of 0.2 to 1.0 mM (Fig. S2). These results suggested that SDs@ZIF-8 had favorable photostability, further demonstrating its prospect for practical analytical application.

To achieve the best sensitivity and precision, the medium pH, the concentration of SDs@ZIF-8 and reaction time were optimized. Fig. S3 shown the effect of pH on the fluorescence change of the analytical method. When pH increased from 4.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence ratio F0/F increased (F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of SDs@ZIF-8 before and after adding tartrazine, respectively), while pH increased from 7.0 to 10.0, the fluorescence ratio F0/F decreased gradually. Therefore, we chosen pH = 7 as the best pH value for the detection of tartrazine. The influence of SDs@ZIF-8 concentration on the fluorescence change of the platform was displayed in Fig. S4. When the concentration of SDs@ZIF-8 gradually increased to 30 mg/mL, the fluorescence ratio F0/F of the system gradually increased to the maximum. However, when the concentration exceeded 30 mg/mL, the fluorescence ratio F0/F decreased. Thus, the concentration of SDs@ZIF-8 was selected to be 30 mg/mL. Further investigation was conducted to examine the effect of reaction time on the detection. Fig. S5 showed that the reaction between SDs@ZIF-8 and tartrazine was fast, taking only 2 min to complete, so the reaction time of the subsequent experiments was set as 2 min.

The obtained SDs@ZIF-8 composites were applied for efficient enrichment and fluorescence detection of tartrazine. Under the best experimental conditions, the dependence of fluorescence ratio F0/F on tartrazine with various concentrations was illustrated in Fig. 5 , where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of SDs@ZIF-8 without and with tartrazine, respectively. As described in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of SDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites gradually decreased with the increase of tartrazine concentration. The linear relationship between F0/F and tartrazine concentration was found to be excellent in the concentration range of 0.02–90 μM(Fig. 5B). The fitted curve was defined as F0/F = 0.01516X + 1.0024 (X represents the concentration of tartrazine, μM), and the correlation coefficient was 0.9921. Based on 3S/K, the detection limit of SDs@ZIF-8 for tartrazine was calculated to be 6.5 nM. Incomparisonwith previously reported results of tartrazine analysis, our methodprovidesalower detection limit andawider range of detection (Table.S1). In addition, our method has three advantages, (1) fast speed, the sensing material and the target can be balanced within 2 min, greatly saving the detection time. (2) High sensitivity, detection limit up to 6.5 nm. (3) The operation is simple and does not require professional technical personnel.

To investigate the selectivity of our developed method, we mixed SDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites with different substances, such as K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, HCO- 3, Cl-, glucose, ascorbic acid, citric acid, lactose, fructose, malachite green, bright blue, amaranth and tartrazine. The fluorescence response results were shown in Fig. 6 . It can be found from Fig. 6A that other substances hardly affect fluorescence of SDs@ZIF-8, only amaranth could slightly reduce fluorescence of the composites. Compared with them, tartrazine could quench more than 80 % of fluorescence intensity of the SDs@ZIF-8. In addition, the anti-interference performance of the system was further investigated by mixing tartrazine with different coexisting substances. As depicted in Fig. 6B, the quenching effect of tartrazine was found to remain unchanged in the presence of other interfering substances, indicating that the determination of tartrazine was not influenced by them. The results suggested that the sensing technique we created exhibited good selectivity in detecting tartrazine.

In addition, the reusability of the tartrazine fluorescent sensor based on SDs@ZIF-8 was investigated. The fluorescence of SDs@ZIF-8 was quenched when tartrazine was added to the dispersion containing SDs@ZIF-8. Subsequently, the mixed solution was centrifuged, and tartrazine was eluted from SDs@ZIF-8. Finally, the fluorescence of the sensor was restored again. The results are shown in Fig. S9, this fluorescence “quenching-recovery” can be repeated for 6 cycles without significant signal degradation. The experimental results show that the sensor has good reusability for the detection of tartrazine.

To understand the theory of fluorescence quenching, we studied the interaction between tartrazine and SDs@ZIF-8. Firstly, we examined the adsorption of SDs@ZIF-8 on tartrazine. The suspension formed by mixing 30 mg of SDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles with 40 μM tartrazine solution appeared yellow (Fig. S6B). Nonetheless, centrifugation resulted in a colorless supernatant, indicating that most of yellow color had been concentrated at the bottom of the centrifuge tube (Fig. S6A). This result demonstrated that SDs@ZIF-8 had high adsorption capacity for tartrazine, which could be utilized for target enrichment, thereby enhancing the sensitivity and selectivity of assay. Next, we investigated the optical properties of the synthesized SDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of tartrazine. As depicted in Fig. S6C and Fig. S6D, the blue luminescence of SDs@ZIF-8 under UV light disappeared after the addition of tartrazine (40 μM). The fluorescence spectra showed a marked decrease in the emission intensity of SDs@ZIF-8 at 426 nm. Furthermore, we measured the UV–Vis absorption spectra of SDs@ZIF-8, tartrazine, and SDs@ZIF-8- tartrazine. As presented in Fig. S7, tartrazine displayed two absorption peaks at 256 and 429 nm. Moreover, no new UV–Vis absorption peaks were observed in SDs@ZIF-8 + tartrazine, indicating that no new complex was formed. Therefore, the reason for the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the system was not due to static quenching. After further investigation, as shown in Fig. 7 , the UV–visible absorption spectrum of tartrazine overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of SDs@ZIF-8, suggesting that the quenching mechanism may be inner filter effect (IFE). To further understand this principle, we conducted fluorescence lifetime measurements. As demonstrated in Fig. S8, the fluorescence decay curves of SDs@ZIF-8 remained superimposed with and without the addition of tartrazine, further indicating that the fluorescence quenching between SDs@ZIF-8 and tartrazine was due to IFE.

To validate the efficacy of this technology, tartrazine in orange juice, cola drinks and hard candy was analyzed. Different concentrations of tartrazine standard solution were added to the real samples and our strategy was employed for measurements. As shown in Table 1 , the average recoveries ranged from 89.5% to 104.6%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 1.57% to 5.81%, implying the high accuracy of the established platform. The results confirmed that the fluorescent probe method we developed was a viable approach for the detection of tartrazine in real samples. Furthermore, tartrazine in the aforementioned samples was also analyzed by HPLC. The analysis results were shown in Table 1. It can be seen that the results obtained by the two methods are very similar, which further illustrates the precision of our method.

In general, we have successfully constructed a new fluorescence sensor (SDs@ZIF-8) for ultrasensitive tartrazine monitoring in food samples. The composites were prepared by embedding sulfur nanodots into ZIF-8 via a facile one-pot approach. The SDs@ZIF-8 composites exhibited high adsorption efficiency and exceptional fluorescence performances. The adsorption of tartrazine by SDs@ZIF-8 was high, and the fluorescence emission of SDs@ZIF-8 declined as tartrazine concentration enhanced. The detection limit of SDs@ZIF-8 for tartrazine was as low as 6.5 nM. The selectivity of the method was good, and only amaranth slightly reduced the fluorescence of the composites. Furthermore, the developed sensor was successfully utilized to identify tartrazine in different food samples. The established method is convenient, rapid, sensitive and selective, and has potential for further applications in the testing of other target molecules in the food security and environmental protection industries.

This research is supported by Shandong Natural Science Foundation (ZR2022MB118), Weifang University of Science and Technology Fund (2021XKJS39, 2022KJ06), The Project of Weifang Science and Technology Development Plan (2022GX038) and Weifang University of Science and Technology High-level Talent Research Initiation Project (2018RC004).

Haijian Liu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Miao Wang: Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Guofu Huang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123187.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step electrochemical approach for the preparation of graphene wrapped-phosphotungstic acid hybrid and its application for simultaneous determination of sunset yellow and tartrazine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Gan, J. Sun, S. Cao, F. Gao, Y. Zhang, Y. Yang, One-step electrochemical approach for the preparation of graphene wrapped-phosphotungstic acid hybrid and its application for simultaneous determination of sunset yellow and tartrazine, Electrochimica Acta, 74 (2012) 151-157. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2012.04.039

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of Au supported on ionic liquid functionalized reduced graphene oxide for simultaneous determination of Sunset yellow and Tartrazine in drinks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Wang, J. Zhao, Facile synthesis of Au supported on ionic liquid functionalized reduced graphene oxide for simultaneous determination of Sunset yellow and Tartrazine in drinks, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical, 216 (2015) 578-585. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.04.053

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of food azo dyes tartrazine and carmoisine on biochemical parameters related to renal, hepatic function and oxidative stress biomarkers in young male rats

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.A. Amin, H.A. Hameid, II, A.H. Abd Elsttar, Effect of food azo dyes tartrazine and carmoisine on biochemical parameters related to renal, hepatic function and oxidative stress biomarkers in young male rats, Food and Chemical Toxicology, 48 (2010) 2994-2999. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fct.2010.07.039

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical synthesis of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) on terbium hexacyanoferrate for sensitive determination of tartrazine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sakthivel, M. Sivakumar, S.-M. Chen, K. Pandi, Electrochemical synthesis of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) on terbium hexacyanoferrate for sensitive determination of tartrazine, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical, 256 (2018) 195-203. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.086

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a highly sensitive fluorescence method for tartrazine determination in food matrices based on carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Liu, H. Sun, L. Xiao, Z.-q. Yang, J. Han, X. Gong, Q. Hu, Development of a highly sensitive fluorescence method for tartrazine determination in food matrices based on carbon dots, Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry, 413 (2021) 1485-1492. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00216-020-03118-1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A nanosilver-based spectrophotometry method for sensitive determination of tartrazine in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Sahraei, A. Farmany, S.S. Mortazavi, A nanosilver-based spectrophotometry method for sensitive determination of tartrazine in food samples, Food Chemistry, 138 (2013) 1239-1242. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2012.11.029

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous Spectrophotometric Determination of Brilliant Blue and Tartrazine in Diverse Sample Matrices after Solid Phase Extraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Bisgin, Simultaneous Spectrophotometric Determination of Brilliant Blue and Tartrazine in Diverse Sample Matrices after Solid Phase Extraction, Journal of AOAC International, 103 (2020) 1478-1485. https://doi.org/10.1093/jaoacint/qsaa056

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast chromatographic method for the determination of dyes in beverages by using high performance liquid chromatography-Diode array detection data and second order algorithms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Culzoni, A.V. Schenone, N.E. Llamas, M. Garrido, M.S. Di Nezio, B.S. Fernandez Band, H.C. sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step solvent exfoliation of graphite to produce a highly-sensitive electrochemical sensor for tartrazine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Song, Z. Shi, X. Tan, S. Zhang, G. Liu, K. Wu, One-step solvent exfoliation of graphite to produce a highly-sensitive electrochemical sensor for tartrazine, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical, 197 (2014) 104-108. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.02.064

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of dyes in foodstuffs by capillary zone electrophoresis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive, selective, and rapid method for optical recognition of ultra-traces level of Hg(II), Ag(I), Au(III), and Pd(II) in electronic wastes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Shahat, S. Trupp, Sensitive, selective, and rapid method for optical recognition of ultra-traces level of Hg(II), Ag(I), Au(III), and Pd(II) in electronic wastes, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical, 245 (2017) 789-802. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.008

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical recognition and removal of Hg(II) using a new self-chemosensor based on a modified amino-functionalized Al-MOF
